chunks.title: WHO BMI Classification for Adults
chunks.content:
BMI is calculated as weight(kg) / height(m)². WHO BMI categories for adults (20+ years):
- Severe Thinness: < 16.0
- Moderate Thinness: 16.0 – 16.9
- Mild Thinness: 17.0 – 18.4
- Normal Weight: 18.5 – 24.9
- Overweight (Pre-obese): 25.0 – 29.9
- Obese Class I: 30.0 – 34.9
- Obese Class II: 35.0 – 39.9
- Obese Class III (Extreme): ≥ 40.0
Note: For Asian populations, overweight starts at BMI 23 and obese at BMI 25 due to higher metabolic risk at lower BMI. Source: WHO Technical Report Series 894, NIH NHLBI.

chunks.title: Ideal Body Weight by Height (BMI 22 Target)
chunks.content:
Ideal body weight at BMI 22 (healthy midpoint) for various heights:
Height 140cm → 43.1 kg
Height 145cm → 46.2 kg
Height 150cm → 49.5 kg
Height 155cm → 52.9 kg
Height 160cm → 56.3 kg
Height 165cm → 59.9 kg
Height 170cm → 63.6 kg
Height 175cm → 67.4 kg
Height 180cm → 71.3 kg
Height 185cm → 75.3 kg
Height 190cm → 79.4 kg
Height 195cm → 83.7 kg
Height 200cm → 88.0 kg
Formula: Ideal Weight (kg) = 22 × (height in meters)². Same for men and women. Source: WHO, NIH NHLBI.

chunks.title: BMI Limitations and Additional Assessments
chunks.content:
BMI does not measure body fat directly. Limitations:
- Muscular individuals may have high BMI but low body fat.
- Older adults may have 'normal' BMI but high body fat (sarcopenic obesity).
- BMI doesn't account for fat distribution — abdominal fat is higher risk.
Additional assessments to consider:
- Waist circumference: Men >102cm, Women >88cm indicates elevated risk (NIH).
- Waist-to-hip ratio: Men >0.9, Women >0.85 indicates risk (WHO).
- Body fat percentage: Men 18-25%, Women 25-31% = acceptable (ACE). Source: NIH NHLBI, WHO.
